meta.title: Coffee Business Vocabulary — Spaced Repetition Deck
meta.method: Leitner box spaced repetition (5 boxes: 1/2/4/7/14 day intervals). Box 1 = MCQ recognition. Box 2+ = fill-in-the-blank recall.

cards.definition: Farmers forming their own company to pool coffee and sell it together as one unit instead of as separate, weak sellers.
cards.analogy: Like a housing society, but for selling coffee instead of managing a building.
cards.why_it_matters: If BR Hills' Soliga farmers already have one, you deal with one supplier instead of fifteen individual people.
cards.cloze: An ___ is farmers forming their own mini company to pool and sell coffee together as one unit.

cards.definition: The umbrella category of farmer collectives — could be structured as an FPC, a cooperative society, or a trust.
cards.analogy: FPO is the category 'vehicle'; FPC is one specific model of vehicle.
cards.why_it_matters: When Coffee Board or VGKK say 'farmer organisation,' ask which structure it actually is — it changes how they negotiate.
cards.cloze: ___ is the umbrella term that FPC sits under — it could be a company, cooperative, or trust.

cards.definition: An older, simpler legal structure than an FPC — members pool produce and share profits under Cooperative Societies law, often with stronger state involvement.
cards.analogy: Closer to how Araku's Girijan Cooperative Society is set up.
cards.why_it_matters: The Araku model you're studying is a cooperative, not an FPC — different governance, worth understanding before trying to replicate it in Shevaroys.
cards.cloze: The Araku Girijan model is a ___, governed under Cooperative Societies law rather than Companies law.

cards.definition: A small group (often 10–20 women) who save and lend together and increasingly run livelihood activities.
cards.analogy: A trust circle that's now also becoming a business unit.
cards.why_it_matters: If you ever explore a women-led retail angle, SHGs are existing infrastructure Coffee Board is already building — not something to create from scratch.
cards.cloze: An ___ is a small savings-and-lending group of women, often now running livelihood businesses like café kiosks.

cards.definition: National Bank for Agriculture and Rural Development — funds and supports FPC formation with working-capital loans and subsidies.
cards.analogy: The government's rural-development bank.
cards.why_it_matters: If a farmer group doesn't yet have an FPC, NABARD is often why one could exist within a year or two.
cards.cloze: ___ is the government rural-development bank that funds and subsidises the formation of FPCs.

cards.definition: Official Coffee Board registration proving you're a legitimate exporter. Valid 3 years.
cards.analogy: Like a gym membership card, but for being a recognized exporter.
cards.why_it_matters: Without it you can't formally export; with it, customs and international buyers recognize you as legitimate.
cards.cloze: An ___ is the Coffee Board certificate that officially recognizes you as an exporter, valid for 3 years.

cards.definition: Required license to operate a curing works — the facility that turns raw cherry/parchment into clean, exportable green beans.
cards.analogy: Like an FSSAI license, but coffee-specific.
cards.why_it_matters: You won't run curing yourself, but it tells you the right question to ask toll roasters and curing works: 'are you licensed, and at what scale?'
cards.cloze: A ___ is required to operate a curing works that hulls, polishes, grades and sorts coffee into green beans.

cards.definition: Registration needed to run pulping equipment at scale — pulping strips skin and pulp off fresh cherry, usually within hours of picking.
cards.analogy: The very first processing step after picking.
cards.why_it_matters: If Shevaroys smallholders pulp their own cherry rather than selling wet cherry to a central point, that signals a more independent, quality-controlled farmer — a good storytelling detail.
cards.cloze: Pulping strips the skin off fresh cherry within hours of picking; running one at scale needs a ___.

cards.definition: Runs the weekly Wednesday–Thursday Bengaluru coffee auction — a public price-discovery mechanism.
cards.analogy: The 'mandi' for coffee.
cards.why_it_matters: ICTA prices are your best public benchmark for whether a farmer's asking price is fair, without seeming like you're haggling.
cards.cloze: ___ runs the weekly Bengaluru coffee auction that sets public benchmark prices like 'Plantation A' and 'Arabica cherry AB.'

cards.definition: The global body that tracks and reports coffee statistics across producing and consuming countries — source of the I-CIP price.
cards.analogy: Coffee's version of OPEC's reporting arm, but without price-setting power.
cards.why_it_matters: It only measures and reports — it doesn't set prices, unlike a cartel.
cards.cloze: The ___ is the global body that reports coffee statistics and publishes the I-CIP price, but does not set prices itself.

cards.term: Cherry → Parchment → Green Bean → Clean Coffee
cards.definition: The bean passes through a fixed sequence of stages, each stripping away more of the fruit — starting as the whole ripe fruit and ending as sorted, defect-free green coffee. Price per kg rises sharply at each step since more has been removed.
cards.analogy: Each stage strips away more of the fruit, so price per kg rises sharply at each step.
cards.why_it_matters: When a farmer quotes a price, always ask which stage it's for — cherry is cheapest by weight since so much gets removed later.
cards.cloze: The bean's stages in order are: Cherry → ___ → Green Bean → Clean Coffee.

cards.definition: Cherries are pulped the same day, then mucilage is broken down in fermentation tanks before washing and drying — produces a cleaner, brighter cup.
cards.analogy: This is Galaxy Frog's profile.
cards.why_it_matters: This is the benchmark 'clean' process against which honey and natural coffees are judged.
cards.cloze: The ___ process pulps cherry the same day, ferments off the mucilage, then washes and dries — giving a bright, clean cup.

cards.definition: Whole cherries are dried in the sun with fruit still intact, then hulled afterward — fruit sugars ferment into the bean, giving heavier body and fruitier flavours.
cards.analogy: The opposite extreme from washed processing.
cards.why_it_matters: Understanding this vs. washed helps you describe why a Seasonal-tier lot tastes the way it does.
cards.cloze: In ___ processing, whole cherries dry in the sun with the fruit intact, producing heavier body and wilder flavours.

cards.definition: A middle path: skin is removed but some or all mucilage is left on during drying. Named white/yellow/red/black by how much remains.
cards.analogy: A dial between fully washed and fully natural.
cards.why_it_matters: This is the 'monitored natural/honey' approach flagged for the Seasonal tier's first season.
cards.cloze: In ___ processing, skin is removed but mucilage is left on during drying — named white/yellow/red/black by how much remains.

cards.definition: Counts physical defects per sample: Category 1 = serious (black, sour, insect-damaged beans); Category 2 = minor (broken, husk fragments, immature).
cards.analogy: A checkable score card for quality, not a vague impression.
cards.why_it_matters: A concrete, falsifiable metric you can ask a farmer or curing works to report — fits the 'named farm, named price, named quality' brand ethos better than a vague 'good quality' claim.
cards.cloze: ___ grading separates serious defects (Category 1) like black/sour beans from minor ones (Category 2) like broken beans.

cards.definition: A person certified through a rigorous multi-day exam to score green coffee on the standardised SCA 100-point cupping scale.
cards.analogy: Like a wine sommelier's certification, but for coffee cupping.
cards.why_it_matters: A formal cupping score on a Seasonal-tier bag (e.g. 'cupped at 84.5') needs to come from a Q Grader to carry real credibility.
cards.cloze: A ___ is certified to score coffee consistently on the SCA 100-point scale — the coffee world's sommelier equivalent.

cards.definition: Green coffee scoring at or above the industry's specialty cutoff on the 100-point SCA scale is "specialty grade"; below that, it's commercial/commodity grade regardless of origin story.
cards.analogy: A hard, checkable number, unlike marketing language.
cards.why_it_matters: This is the industry-standard bar your Seasonal-tier coffee needs to clear to legitimately call itself specialty.
cards.cloze: Coffee scoring ___ or above on the SCA 100-point scale is considered specialty grade.

cards.definition: Seller's price/responsibility ends once goods are loaded onto the ship at the origin port; buyer pays sea freight and insurance from there.
cards.analogy: You hand the bag over at the dock; they take it from there.
cards.why_it_matters: When quoting The Wild to an overseas buyer, FOB (Chennai/Cochin) vs. CIF changes your price by a meaningful margin.
cards.cloze: ___ pricing means the seller's responsibility ends once goods are loaded onto the ship at the origin port.

cards.definition: Seller's quoted price already includes shipping and insurance all the way to the destination port.
cards.analogy: Door-to-door pricing, vs. FOB's dock-only pricing.
cards.why_it_matters: Whether you quote FOB or CIF changes who's liable if something goes wrong in transit.
cards.cloze: ___ pricing means the seller's quote already includes shipping and insurance to the destination port.

cards.definition: An internationally standard numeric classification for every traded good, used by customs everywhere (e.g. HS 090111 = green coffee).
cards.analogy: A universal product barcode category every country's customs reads the same way.
cards.why_it_matters: Tariff rates are set per HS code, not per 'coffee' generally — whether you export green, roasted, or instant genuinely changes your duty rate.
cards.cloze: An ___ is the internationally standard numeric classification customs use for every traded good, including coffee.

cards.definition: The standard default import duty a country charges any WTO member without a special trade deal — the baseline unless an agreement like CETA/CEPA lowers it.
cards.analogy: The rate everyone gets unless they have a special deal.
cards.why_it_matters: UK's MFN rate on roast & ground is 6–8%; under CETA, India specifically gets 0% — a real cost advantage over Brazil or Vietnam.
cards.cloze: The ___ tariff is the standard default import duty a country charges, unless a trade agreement lowers it further.

cards.definition: An EU law requiring proof that imported commodities weren't grown on land deforested after a cutoff date, with full supply-chain traceability back to the plot, often GPS-verified.
cards.analogy: The EU saying: 'show us exactly which patch of land this grew on, and prove it was already farmland.'
cards.why_it_matters: Named-farm, named-plot sourcing is exactly the traceability EUDR requires — a genuine tailwind for your model, and why EUDR was flagged as a future demand driver for cacao too.
cards.cloze: The ___ requires GPS-verified, plot-level traceability proving coffee wasn't grown on recently deforested land.

cards.definition: The ICO's single blended reference price for coffee globally, in US cents/lb, weighting different coffee-group prices by their share of world trade.
cards.analogy: Coffee's version of a stock market index like the Nifty 50.
cards.why_it_matters: One number that summarises how the market is doing, even though individual origins move differently underneath it.
cards.cloze: The ___ is the ICO's single blended reference price for coffee globally — like a stock market index for coffee.

cards.definition: The price gap between two coffee categories or lots — for example the gap between Colombian Milds and Robustas. Widening means milder coffees are pulling ahead in price; narrowing means the gap is closing.
cards.analogy: A spread, like the gap between two stock prices.
cards.why_it_matters: Tracking the Arabica-Robusta differential tells you whether The Everyday's Robusta-heavy economics are getting cheaper or pricier relative to Seasonal-tier Arabica sourcing.
cards.cloze: The ___ is the price gap between two coffee categories or lots — useful for tracking Robusta vs. Arabica cost trends.

cards.definition: Coffee graded, warehoused, and officially certified as deliverable against a futures contract on an exchange. Falling stocks signal tightening supply and rising prices; rising stocks signal the opposite.
cards.analogy: The exchange's verified inventory of ready-to-deliver coffee.
cards.why_it_matters: Falling certified stocks is a leading signal that prices may be about to rise.
cards.cloze: ___ are coffee lots graded and warehoused as officially deliverable against a futures contract — falling levels signal tightening supply.

cards.definition: An agreement to buy or sell coffee at a set price for future delivery, locking in the price now regardless of market moves.
cards.analogy: Like pre-booking a hotel room at today's rate for a trip six months out.
cards.why_it_matters: This is the mitigant flagged for green bean price volatility once your volumes justify it — not needed at current small-batch stage.
cards.cloze: A ___ locks in a price now for coffee to be delivered at a future date, regardless of how the market moves.

cards.definition: An informal, non-audited sourcing relationship where the buyer deals directly with the farmer or farmer group, cutting out middlemen, paying a negotiated price rather than a certification-mandated floor.
cards.analogy: This is essentially your model — and Black Baza's.
cards.why_it_matters: It's the foundation your brand's named-farm, named-price claims are built on.
cards.cloze: ___ is an informal sourcing relationship where the buyer deals directly with the farmer, cutting out middlemen.

cards.definition: Paying an existing roaster a per-kg fee to roast your own green beans to your own spec, without owning roasting equipment yourself.
cards.analogy: Like using a shared commercial kitchen to bake your own recipe, rather than buying an oven.
cards.why_it_matters: This is exactly the arrangement with Adrian at GB Roasters and the Blue Tokai Seetharampalya backup.
cards.cloze: ___ means paying an existing roaster a per-kg fee to roast your own beans without owning the equipment.

cards.definition: Shorthand for Hotels, Restaurants, and Cafés — the standard industry term for the B2B foodservice channel, distinct from retail.
cards.analogy: The channel label for The Everyday's target buyers.
cards.why_it_matters: This is the standard term for exactly the channel Bowring Club and Century Club sit in.
cards.cloze: ___ is the industry shorthand for Hotels, Restaurants, and Cafés — the B2B foodservice channel.

cards.definition: The true total cost of a product once it reaches you — bean cost + transport + toll roasting fee + packaging + duties — vs. just the farm-gate price.
cards.analogy: The real number, not the headline price you paid the farmer.
cards.why_it_matters: The ₹700 cost-to-produce-a-kg figure in the Action Plan is essentially a landed cost calculation.
cards.cloze: ___ is the true total cost once a product reaches you — beans, transport, roasting, packaging and duties combined.

cards.definition: The time gap between paying cash out (beans, roaster fees) and receiving cash in (client payment). Longer cycle = more cash reserves needed idle.
cards.analogy: How long your money is 'out' before it comes back to be reused.
cards.why_it_matters: This is exactly what the '15-day invoice terms, 2 months of bean stock' risk mitigant is managing.
cards.cloze: The ___ is the gap between paying cash out for beans/roasting and receiving cash in from client invoices.

cards.definition: The earliest era of mass-market coffee (Folgers, Mr. Coffee) treated as a commodity — cheap, convenient, grab-and-go, with little attention paid to quality or origin.
cards.analogy: Coffee as fuel, like a gas-station fill-up rather than a meal.
cards.why_it_matters: Knowing this baseline helps you recognize how far "just a cup of coffee" has evolved — useful context whenever a café markets itself as different from supermarket coffee.
cards.cloze: The ___ of coffee treated it as a cheap, mass-market commodity with little focus on quality — think Folgers or an old drip machine.

cards.definition: The era defined by Starbucks-style espresso culture — customizable drinks, coffee shops as a social "third place," and the rise of the barista as a public-facing role.
cards.analogy: Coffee becomes an experience and a customizable order, not just fuel.
cards.why_it_matters: This is the wave most people still picture when they think "coffee shop" — recognizing it helps you spot when a café is actually operating in third-wave territory instead.
cards.cloze: ___ coffee is marked by Starbucks-style espresso culture, drink customization, and cafés as a social gathering spot.

cards.definition: The current era treating coffee as a craft — full traceability to origin, labels detailing altitude/variety/processing, and pride in brewing technique.
cards.analogy: Coffee treated more like wine or craft beer than a commodity.
cards.why_it_matters: This is the frame "specialty coffee" sits inside — useful shorthand whenever you're trying to explain why a bag of coffee costs more than the supermarket tin.
cards.cloze: ___ coffee treats the drink as a craft, with full traceability and labels detailing altitude, variety, and processing.

cards.definition: How a coffee's environment — soil, altitude, climate, surrounding farm conditions — shapes its flavor.
cards.analogy: The same idea used to explain why wine tastes different from one hillside to the next.
cards.why_it_matters: Explains why two coffees grown a few miles apart can taste completely different, even with identical processing.
cards.cloze: ___ describes how soil, altitude, and climate shape a coffee's flavor.

cards.definition: A coffee cherry that develops just one round bean instead of the usual two flat-sided beans.
cards.analogy: Like finding a single-yolk egg where you'd normally expect two seeds sharing a pod.
cards.why_it_matters: Explains a term you'll spot on specialty bags and menus, often sold as a distinct, sometimes pricier grade.
cards.cloze: A ___ is a coffee cherry that develops just one round bean instead of the usual two.

cards.definition: A specific coffee variety (genetic cultivar) known for being slow to fruit, expensive to grow, and for producing wild, distinctive floral and tea-like flavors.
cards.analogy: Like a rare apple cultivar prized far above ordinary eating apples.
cards.why_it_matters: The variety most likely to come up when someone talks about a coffee that broke an auction record.
cards.cloze: ___ is a coffee variety famous for wild, distinctive flavors and a high price tag.

cards.definition: Water passes through a bed of grounds (usually via a paper filter), as in drip machines or a V60 pour-over — producing clean, bright cups.
cards.analogy: Water raining straight down through a coffee-filled funnel.
cards.why_it_matters: Explains why a pour-over and a French press can taste so different even with the same beans — the brew category itself shapes the cup.
cards.cloze: ___ brewing passes water through a bed of grounds via a filter, producing clean, bright cups — think drip or V60.

cards.definition: Grounds steep directly in water and are then filtered out (often through metal), producing a thicker, silkier cup with more retained oils — as in a French press.
cards.analogy: Like steeping tea leaves loose in a pot rather than running water through a bag.
cards.why_it_matters: A useful category to reach for when you want a heavier-bodied cup than a pour-over gives you.
cards.cloze: ___ brewing steeps grounds directly in water before filtering them out, producing a thicker, oilier cup — think French press.

cards.definition: Pressurized water is forced through finely-ground, tamped coffee to produce a small, concentrated shot — the base for milk drinks like lattes and cappuccinos. It's a brew method, not a roast level.
cards.analogy: More like a pressure-cooked concentrate than a "type" of bean.
cards.why_it_matters: Clears up a common mix-up — coffee isn't roasted "for espresso" in some fundamentally different way; espresso is how you brew it, not what it is.
cards.cloze: ___ is a brew method where pressurized water is forced through fine, tamped grounds to produce a concentrated shot.

cards.definition: The industry-standard tasting protocol — grind, add hot water, break the crust, taste with a spoon — used worldwide by roasters and graders to score and compare coffees consistently.
cards.analogy: A standardized wine tasting, but for green coffee.
cards.why_it_matters: The method behind every SCA score you see printed on a bag — worth knowing so those numbers feel concrete rather than marketing.
cards.cloze: ___ is the standardized tasting protocol — grind, add water, break the crust, taste — used to score coffee worldwide.

cards.definition: A feature on more advanced espresso machines that lets you vary water pressure through the shot (rather than holding it constant) for finer flavor control.
cards.analogy: Like adjusting your grip pressure through a handshake instead of squeezing at one constant force the whole time.
cards.why_it_matters: A feature worth knowing about if you ever compare higher-end home espresso machines.
cards.cloze: ___ lets you vary water pressure through an espresso shot instead of holding it constant.

cards.definition: Espresso diluted with hot water, in varying ratios — the closest espresso-based drink to a black filter coffee.
cards.analogy: Espresso stretched out with water instead of milk.
cards.why_it_matters: A good order when you want espresso's flavor without milk or a full shot's concentration.
cards.cloze: A ___ is espresso diluted with hot water in varying ratios.

cards.definition: Espresso with just a small dash of milk (roughly an ounce) — barely lightened, not a milk-forward drink.
cards.analogy: Espresso with a splash, not a pour.
cards.why_it_matters: Useful to know before ordering — a macchiato is nowhere near as milky as a latte, and baristas notice when it's ordered by mistake.
cards.cloze: A ___ is espresso with just a small dash of milk, about an ounce.

cards.definition: Roughly equal parts espresso and steamed milk, with little to no foam on top.
cards.analogy: A middle ground in milk ratio — between a bare macchiato and a fuller flat white, without foam.
cards.why_it_matters: A useful order when a straight shot feels too intense but a latte feels too diluted.
cards.cloze: A ___ is roughly equal parts espresso and steamed milk.

cards.definition: About 5–6 oz total — espresso plus steamed milk with thin, silky microfoam and very little air.
cards.analogy: A latte's smaller, denser sibling, and less airy than a cappuccino.
cards.why_it_matters: The drink to order when you want a milk drink without the thick foam layer of a cappuccino.
cards.cloze: A ___ is roughly 5–6 oz of espresso and steamed milk with thin microfoam, less airy than a cappuccino.

cards.definition: About 5–6 oz total — espresso plus steamed milk, traditionally topped with a thick layer of foam.
cards.analogy: A flat white's frothier sibling, with noticeably more foam.
cards.why_it_matters: A good drink to include in the "three-drink test" since it directly tests a barista's milk-texturing skill.
cards.cloze: A ___ is roughly 5–6 oz of espresso and milk, traditionally with more foam on top than a flat white.

cards.definition: Espresso with a generous volume of steamed milk — the catch-all term for bigger, milkier espresso drinks.
cards.analogy: The biggest, mildest of the standard milk drinks — noticeably more milk than a cappuccino or flat white.
cards.why_it_matters: The right order when you want a milkier, more diluted espresso drink rather than a concentrated one.
cards.cloze: A ___ is espresso with a larger volume of steamed milk than a cappuccino or flat white.

cards.definition: An audible popping stage during roasting, after which beans turn oily and take on a heavy, smoky, carbon-forward flavor that masks origin character.
cards.analogy: Like popcorn's second, quieter round of pops.
cards.why_it_matters: A marker for spotting when a roast has been pushed past the point where a coffee's own origin flavor can still shine through.
cards.cloze: ___ is the audible popping stage after which beans turn oily with heavy, smoky flavor.

cards.definition: Freshly roasted coffee holds a lot of CO2, and brewing it too soon after roasting can produce off flavors. A short rest period is generally recommended before brewing, though many coffees still taste great 30+ days off roast.
cards.analogy: Bread dough needing time to rest before baking evenly.
cards.why_it_matters: Explains why "freshest possible" isn't always "best possible" when buying a just-roasted bag.
cards.cloze: A rest period of roughly ___ days after roasting is generally recommended before brewing.

cards.definition: Not enough flavor is pulled from the grounds (too little water, too coarse a grind, or too short a brew time), producing sour, puckering flavors.
cards.analogy: Tea steeped for too short a time — thin and sharp rather than balanced.
cards.why_it_matters: The first thing to check when a cup tastes sour or thin rather than balanced.
cards.cloze: ___ happens when too little is pulled from the grounds, producing sour, puckering flavors.

cards.definition: Too much is pulled from the grounds (too much water, too fine a grind, or too long a brew time), producing bitterness and a lingering harsh finish.
cards.analogy: Tea steeped far too long — bitter and harsh rather than balanced.
cards.why_it_matters: The first thing to check when a cup tastes bitter or harsh rather than balanced.
cards.cloze: ___ happens when too much is pulled from the grounds, producing bitterness and a lingering finish.

cards.definition: The ongoing process of tasting a brew and adjusting parameters — most often grind size — to hit a target result, typically tracked via input (dose), output (yield), and time.
cards.analogy: Tuning a guitar string by ear, checking and adjusting until it's right.
cards.why_it_matters: The habit that separates a consistently good cup from a lucky one-off.
cards.cloze: ___ is the process of tasting and adjusting grind size and other parameters to hit a target brew.

cards.definition: The reddish-brown foam layer on top of a fresh espresso shot, caused by trapped gas — a sign of freshness, though not flavorful on its own.
cards.analogy: The head on a freshly poured beer.
cards.why_it_matters: Explains why a shot's top layer looks the way it does — worth knowing so it isn't mistaken for a flavor indicator.
cards.cloze: ___ is the foam layer on top of espresso, caused by trapped gas from fresh coffee.

cards.definition: Using fine needles to break up clumps in the espresso puck before tamping, to prevent channeling and promote even extraction.
cards.analogy: Combing out tangles before braiding hair, so everything runs smooth and even.
cards.why_it_matters: A small habit that noticeably improves shot consistency if you're troubleshooting uneven espresso.
cards.cloze: ___ uses fine needles to break up clumps in the espresso puck and prevent channeling.

cards.definition: Uneven paths of water through the coffee bed during espresso extraction, causing simultaneous over- and under-extraction in the same shot.
cards.analogy: Water finding a crack in a dam instead of flowing evenly across it.
cards.why_it_matters: The usual culprit behind a shot that tastes both sour and bitter at once.
cards.cloze: ___ is uneven water flow through the coffee bed, causing inconsistent extraction.

cards.term: TDS / EY (Total Dissolved Solids / Extraction Yield)
cards.definition: Measurements taken with a refractometer to objectively quantify how strong a brew is and what percentage of the grounds ended up in the cup.
cards.analogy: A precise thermometer reading instead of guessing "it feels about right."
cards.why_it_matters: Turns "does this taste balanced" into a number you can actually track and repeat.
cards.cloze: ___ are refractometer measurements used to objectively quantify how a coffee was extracted.

cards.definition: Ordering an espresso, a cappuccino, and a filter brew at a new café to judge extraction skill, milk-texturing skill, and how the coffee tastes unmasked, respectively.
cards.analogy: Judging a restaurant by tasting its simplest dish, its signature dish, and its dessert.
cards.why_it_matters: A quick, repeatable way to size up a new café without needing to be an expert taster yourself.
cards.cloze: The ___ means ordering an espresso, cappuccino, and filter brew to judge a café's overall skill.

cards.definition: Unlike old "kitchen sink" commodity blends, a well-made combination of two or more high-quality, traceable coffees can create a new flavor profile — often chocolatey and round, well-suited to espresso with milk.
cards.analogy: Blending two excellent single-malt whiskies on purpose, rather than mixing whatever's cheap.
cards.why_it_matters: Worth knowing before writing off blends altogether — a well-made one is a deliberate flavor choice, not a corner-cutting move.
cards.cloze: ___ combine two or more high-quality, traceable coffees into a new flavor profile, unlike old commodity blends.

cards.definition: A 12 oz drip coffee and a double shot of espresso don't contain the same total caffeine — despite espresso being far more concentrated per ounce, its total serving size is much smaller.
cards.analogy: A shot glass of a strong spirit vs. a full glass of a milder drink — strength and total amount aren't the same thing.
cards.why_it_matters: A common myth worth correcting before assuming an espresso-based drink is always the "stronger" caffeine choice.
cards.cloze: A 12 oz drip coffee typically has ___ total caffeine than a double shot of espresso.

cards.definition: The standard, largest-screen-size bean grade — the reference-quality benchmark most often quoted at auction.
cards.analogy: The ‘regular size’ tier that everything else is measured against.
cards.why_it_matters: ‘AB’ in a price table is the anchor grade — a good baseline for what to expect to pay.
cards.cloze: ___ is the standard, largest-screen-size grade — the benchmark quoted at auction.

cards.definition: A smaller, rounder bean grade that comes from a cherry that developed just one bean instead of the usual two — generally considered a premium grade.
cards.analogy: The ‘special edition’ bean shape, sold as its own category.
cards.why_it_matters: Explains why some bags specifically call out ‘peaberry’ as a distinct, sometimes pricier grade.
cards.cloze: ___ grade beans come from a cherry that developed just one round bean instead of the usual two.

cards.definition: A bean grade with more physical defects than the standard grade — a step down in quality and price.
cards.analogy: The ‘seconds’ tier below the standard size.
cards.why_it_matters: Useful for recognizing when a quoted price is for a lower grade than the reference benchmark.
cards.cloze: ___ grade beans are smaller and carry more defects than the standard benchmark grade.

cards.definition: Ungraded coffee — the lowest tier, not sorted by size or defect count.
cards.analogy: The catch-all bin at the bottom of the sorting process.
cards.why_it_matters: The grade to watch out for if a quoted price seems unusually low — it may not be sorted at all.
cards.cloze: ___ grade coffee is ungraded — the lowest tier, not sorted by size or defect count.

cards.definition: An official third-party certification that guarantees a minimum floor price plus a social premium paid to the farmer group.
cards.analogy: A guaranteed minimum wage, certified by an outside auditor.
cards.why_it_matters: Worth knowing as a baseline — many specialty roasters already pay well above this floor without needing the certification.
cards.cloze: ___ is an official certification guaranteeing a minimum floor price plus a social premium to the farmer group.

cards.definition: A third-party certification that audits environmental and social farming standards, such as land use and worker conditions.
cards.analogy: An outside inspector checking a farm's environmental and labor practices against a checklist.
cards.why_it_matters: One of the certifications that matters more in export contexts than in direct, named-farm relationships.
cards.cloze: ___ certification audits environmental and social farming standards on the farm.

cards.definition: India's official organic certification, confirming the absence of synthetic inputs like chemical fertilizers or pesticides.
cards.analogy: The paperwork proving a farm didn't use synthetic chemicals — not a taste guarantee.
cards.why_it_matters: Many farmers grow organically without paying for this certification — worth checking with a farmer rather than assuming either way.
cards.cloze: ___ is India's official certification confirming a farm didn't use synthetic fertilizers or pesticides.

cards.definition: Coffee traced to one country or region — the broadest level of provenance, which could still span hundreds of individual farms.
cards.analogy: Knowing which country a fruit came from, without knowing which orchard.
cards.why_it_matters: The loosest traceability claim — a good baseline, but not proof of any single farm's identity.
cards.cloze: ___ coffee traces to one country or region, though it could still come from hundreds of different farms.

cards.definition: A small, segregated batch of coffee from one farm or one section of a farm, kept separate rather than blended with other farmers' harvests.
cards.analogy: One specific orchard's harvest, kept in its own crate instead of mixed into the general truckload.
cards.why_it_matters: A step more specific than single-origin — worth knowing when a bag claims more traceability than just a country name.
cards.cloze: A ___ is a small, segregated batch of coffee from one farm or one section of a farm.

cards.definition: A micro-lot with the specific farm, plot, elevation, and farmer's name attached — the strongest, most falsifiable provenance claim.
cards.analogy: The most zoomed-in level of the same map — down to the exact plot.
cards.why_it_matters: The strongest claim a brand can make — because it can be checked and disproven if false.
cards.cloze: A ___ is a micro-lot with the specific farm, plot, elevation, and farmer's name attached — the strongest provenance claim.

cards.definition: The general umbrella term for a bilateral or regional deal between countries that lowers tariffs on trade between them.
cards.analogy: The general category of deal; CEPA and CETA are two specific examples of it.
cards.why_it_matters: Useful shorthand whenever any two countries strike a tariff-lowering deal, regardless of what they specifically call it.
cards.cloze: An ___ is the general term for a deal between countries that lowers tariffs on trade between them.

cards.definition: India's trade agreement with countries like Oman and the UAE — broader than a typical tariff deal since it also covers services.
cards.analogy: One specific brand-name version of a free trade agreement.
cards.why_it_matters: Relevant whenever quoting a price to a buyer in Oman or the UAE — it may unlock a lower duty rate.
cards.cloze: India's trade deal with Oman and the UAE is called ___.

cards.definition: The India–UK version of a free trade agreement, lowering tariffs between the two countries.
cards.analogy: The UK-specific version of the same kind of deal as CEPA.
cards.why_it_matters: Relevant whenever quoting a price to a UK buyer — it can mean a significantly lower duty rate than the standard MFN rate.
cards.cloze: The India-UK version of a free trade agreement is called ___.

cards.definition: A deal where you supply a product that carries someone else's brand name, with no visible trace of your own brand.
cards.analogy: An invisible supplier — your name never appears on the package.
cards.why_it_matters: Useful to recognize when a B2B deal builds no brand equity of your own, even if the volume is good.
cards.cloze: In a ___ deal, the product carries someone else's brand name with no visible trace of the actual supplier.

cards.definition: A deal where the product carries your own brand name and story, even when it's sold business-to-business rather than direct to consumers.
cards.analogy: Your name stays on the bag no matter who's buying it.
cards.why_it_matters: Builds brand equity even from a wholesale sale, unlike a private-label arrangement.
cards.cloze: In a ___ B2B deal, the product carries your own brand name and story even when sold wholesale.

cards.definition: India's tax registration, required once turnover crosses a threshold or for cross-state and B2B sales.
cards.analogy: The standard tax registration nearly every growing business eventually needs.
cards.why_it_matters: Worth checking early — institutional buyers often require a GST invoice before they'll place an order.
cards.cloze: ___ is India's tax registration required for cross-state or B2B sales once turnover crosses a threshold.

cards.definition: The food safety license required for anyone manufacturing or selling a food product, including coffee.
cards.analogy: Like a health department permit for any food business.
cards.why_it_matters: Worth checking early with institutional buyers, since this registration can have real lead time.
cards.cloze: ___ is the food safety license required for anyone manufacturing or selling a food product, including coffee.

cards.definition: Registers a business as a small enterprise, which can unlock scheme eligibility and preference from institutional buyers.
cards.analogy: A ‘small business’ status badge that can open doors with bigger buyers.
cards.why_it_matters: Worth having on file before pitching institutional buyers who may prefer or require MSME-registered vendors.
cards.cloze: ___ registration marks a business as a small enterprise, useful for scheme eligibility and institutional-buyer preference.

cards.definition: A grinder that crushes beans between two surfaces to a consistent size, which specialty brewing generally assumes you're using.
cards.analogy: Like a mill grinding grain to a uniform size.
cards.why_it_matters: The single highest-leverage equipment upgrade for anyone starting to brew more carefully at home.
cards.cloze: A ___ crushes beans between two surfaces to a consistent size.

cards.definition: A grinder that chops beans unevenly with a spinning cutting edge, producing inconsistent particle sizes that undermine extraction.
cards.analogy: More like a blender randomly chopping whatever's inside, rather than a precise mill.
cards.why_it_matters: The equipment to move away from first, if you're serious about improving your home brewing.
cards.cloze: A ___ chops beans unevenly with a spinning cutting edge, undermining consistent extraction.

cards.definition: A burr shape that tends to produce round, thick, creamy coffee.
cards.analogy: One particular knife shape, suited to a rounder cut.
cards.why_it_matters: Useful when shopping for a grinder and trying to predict the flavor tendency it'll favor.
cards.cloze: A ___ burr grinder is generally associated with rounder, thicker, creamier coffee.

cards.definition: A burr shape that tends to produce brighter, more acidic, “modern”-style coffee.
cards.analogy: A different knife shape, suited to a sharper, brighter cut.
cards.why_it_matters: Useful for explaining why two shops' espresso can taste so different even with similar beans.
cards.cloze: A ___ burr grinder is generally associated with brighter, more acidic coffee.

cards.definition: An espresso machine that shares one boiler between brewing and steaming milk, requiring a temperature switch between the two tasks.
cards.analogy: One kettle used for two different jobs, one after the other.
cards.why_it_matters: Explains why some home espresso setups feel slow between pulling a shot and steaming milk.
cards.cloze: A ___ espresso machine shares one boiler between brewing and steaming, requiring a temperature switch.

cards.definition: An espresso machine that separates brewing and steaming into two boilers, letting both happen at their own ideal temperature without switching.
cards.analogy: Two dedicated kettles running at once instead of sharing one.
cards.why_it_matters: Explains why café machines can pull a shot and steam milk back-to-back without any delay.
cards.cloze: A ___ espresso machine separates brewing and steaming into two boilers for a smoother workflow.

cards.definition: A market condition where the spot price is higher than future delivery prices, signalling immediate scarcity.
cards.analogy: “Buy now, don't wait” — prices are only expected to fall or stay flat.
cards.why_it_matters: A signal that locking in supply now, rather than waiting, is the economically rational move.
cards.cloze: In ___, the spot price is higher than future delivery prices, signalling immediate scarcity.

cards.definition: A market condition where future delivery prices are higher than the spot price, signalling comfortable current supply.
cards.analogy: “No rush” — the market expects supply to stay easy for now.
cards.why_it_matters: The opposite signal from backwardation — a cue that waiting to buy may not cost you anything.
cards.cloze: In ___, future delivery prices are higher than the spot price, signalling comfortable current supply.

cards.definition: The numeric result of a formal cupping session on the SCA's 100-point scale — a measure of how good a coffee is.
cards.analogy: A single number, like a review score out of 100.
cards.why_it_matters: The concrete number behind a claim like “cupped at 84.5” — distinct from marketing language.
cards.cloze: A ___ is the numeric SCA result on the 100-point scale, answering “how good is it.”

cards.definition: Descriptive language like “gooseberry” or “tamarind” that describes what a coffee tastes like — storytelling language, not a formal metric.
cards.analogy: The flavor words on a wine label, not the wine's rating.
cards.why_it_matters: Useful, but not a substitute for a formal cup score — the two answer different questions.
cards.cloze: A ___ is descriptive language like “gooseberry” or “tamarind,” answering “what does it taste like.”
